cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Caodong
cb:div.head: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Caodong
cb:div.p:
The uncut jade of Jing Mountain, if it does not meet an enlightened age and Master He,
remains hidden in the stones of Jing, and how could its resplendent, city-worth beauty be revealed? Jade is truly not lacking, but an enlightened age
and a Master He are indeed hard to encounter. The recorded sayings of Xinfeng Heyu
from the beginning onward spread widely in the world. Fish-eyes were mixed with pearls, pure gold lay in the sand. It was fitting that
Venerable Mo deeply lamented this grave abuse, and from Master Guo's Record of the Five Houses and the various texts
he selected the refined and discarded the crude, and at last the record was completed. The Venerable and I
have long had a deep friendship beyond the worldly sphere, and he sent it to me to read. I said: Venerable, are you perhaps
the Master He for Xinfeng's jade? Yet even if there is a Master He, still he may not be encountered.

enlightened age, then how could it have reached this point? Ah, how splendid. Our Great East's
signs of civilization have come to possess such beauty today. As followers of the gate of Confucius, how could we
dare not admire what is admirable and rejoice in what is joyful? The record of Xinfeng has already
been carved into blocks and completed. The record of Heyu is now also completed. I, unworthy as I am, write what makes it worthy of admiration and joy.
Thus may broad-minded people know the elder's true Dharma eye.
Genbun, gengshen year, fifteenth day of the eighth month
Respectfully written by Yanagisawa Satokyo of Kōriyama at the south window of the Green Bamboo Studio

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Caoshan
cb:div.head: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Caoshan
cb:div.p:
The ancients said, The meaning is not in the words. They also said, Once the meaning is grasped, the words are forgotten. Meaning
is the gist. Words are the marker. The gist is not easy to obtain; the marker is not hard to obtain.
Therefore one borrows the easily obtained marker to attain the hard-to-attain gist. If one obtains the gist,
then forgetting the marker is fine. But if the gist is lost, what use is the marker? Thus the ancients, regarding those who only
cling to the marker, called it entangling vines, and also called it a roof tile used to knock at a door.
The Chan practitioner Qiyi Yimo of Yunzhou obtained Master Heyu's sayings from Guo Zhengzhong's
Record of the Five Houses, and also obtained old compilations written by Huixia, Guanghui, Huiran, and others.
He collated agreements and differences, examined and verified the genuine and the spurious, titled it Recorded Sayings of Caoshan, and appended it to the previously
printed Recorded Sayings of Dongshan in order to broaden its transmission. Great indeed is this resolve. From a humble beginning,
it starts. Yet I have not truly known him. Has he indeed grasped the meaning, or is he still clinging to words,
taking them as mere tiles for knocking at the gate? Please ask this Chan practitioner himself.
In the spring of the xinyou year of Kanpo, at the request of the host Genkō of Eagle Peak, I was asked for these remarks; with reverence
I bow my head and compose this.

Empty and mysterious, unattached; wondrously turning the numinous pivot. The golden crow soars by night; the wooden horse neighs in the wind. The four
prohibitions and three falls, a rhinoceros horn lit in the palm. Repeatedly parting the five signs, mud carved from the tip of the nose. Qingyuan's
Baijia wine makes the drunk lose east and west. Eyebrows and eyes no longer know one another, and yet the master still
remains confused. Having once passed through the land of insect poison, even a drop of water is hard to moisten with. If this is drawn out,
what of that one? As it is spread abroad, one must still discern its outlines.
Inscribed with reverence by the śramaṇa Yuanzhi of Linquan

cb:div.cb:mulu: Author's Preface to the Recompiled Recorded Sayings of Great Master Yuanzheng of Caoshan
cb:div.head: Author's Preface to the Recompiled Recorded Sayings of Great Master Yuanzheng of Caoshan
cb:div.p:
What is a recorded sayings text? It is what Great Master Heyu Yuanzheng spoke. What he spoke
exists in ancient copies and exists in present copies. Taking what exists in the present and collating what exists
in the ancient, then the ancient may be acceptable, while the present may not yet be. But if one is to take from them,
should one take the ancient and call it right? Or take the present and call it right? Or rather take both present and ancient
together as right? Alas, what to take and what to discard? Better to collate present and ancient and take
what is acceptable. In general, texts circulating in the world under the title Recorded Sayings of the Great Master are mostly
spurious compilations. For example, in making sermons to the assembly, they may take something like the verse on the Four Prohibitions and add explanatory
words, presenting it as a sermon; from this, one can know the extent of spurious fabrication. As for the recorded sayings of Dong and Cao
in China, they were compiled by Guo Limei. These too are present copies, and
not yet truly ancient. Even so, though not a perfect jade disk, their luster is not of the sort of worthless Yanshi stones
either. Therefore I, unworthy as I am, extracted Master Heyu's teachings from the Record of the Five Houses, taking
what should be taken and discarding what should be discarded, and at times using old compilations to fill in what was lacking. Thus the recorded sayings were completed.
Ah, when the ancients left teachings for later ages, those who bore their intent acted before us.
Their signs and teachings were handed down. We who dwell in later times and bear their intent, how can we stand before them? I,
unworthy as I am, therefore wish to gather the ancients' pivotal sayings and correct the true and
false of old and new, to spread their teaching more widely and diligently. I venture to ask those in later ages who bear their
intent: act for it, enter into it, embody it. If they do
so, it may be said that I and the patriarchs ride the same turtle, drifting and swimming
together in the deep pond. Would that not be delightful?

Winter of the gengshen year, fifth year of Genbun, śramaṇa Yimo Xuanqi of Great Japan
reverently bows and composes this

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Benji of Caoshan in Fuzhou, Volume One

body.byline: Compiled and edited by Guo Ningzhi, Master of the Owner of No Land

cb:div.cb:mulu: Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Caoshan Benji
cb:div.p:
The master's taboo name was Benji. He was a son of the Huang family of Putian, Quanzhou. In youth he studied Confucian learning. At nineteen,
he went to Lingshi in Fuzhou and left home. At twenty-five he received the precepts. Soon he went to see Dongshan. Dong-
shan asked, What is your name, reverend? The master said, Benji. Dongshan said, Say something higher than that.
The master said, I will not say. Dongshan said, Why will you not say? The master said, It is not
named Benji. Dongshan thought highly of him, and from this point onIn the Sengbao biography, the master’s name is Danzhang. Since this Lamp Record records it so, it is followed here.
he entered the inner room. After staying for several years, he took leave. Dongshan then secretly transmitted to him the essential purport of the Dong lineage.
He further asked, Where are you going? The master said, To the place of no change.
Dongshan said, In the place of no change, how can there be going? The master said, Going also
is no change. He then went to Caoqi, paid homage at the patriarch's stupa, and returned to Jishui. The people, hearing of the master's
fame, invited him to open the Dharma. The master admired the Sixth Patriarch in aspiration, and so named the mountain Cao. Soon he encountered
bandit unrest, and then went to Yihuang. There was a faithful layman, Wang Ruoyi, who offered the Hewang Monastery and invited
the master to reside there as abbot. The master renamed Hewang as Heyu. From this, the Dharma seat greatly flourished, and students
gathered like clouds. Dongshan's school reached great prosperity with this master.

Dualistic thinking - whether ordinary or enlightened - is golden shackles on the mysterious path. You must directly realize guest and hostThe verb rendered here as “changing places” translates 回互 (lit. “reciprocally turning/exchanging”). Elsewhere in the Caoshan records, Caoshan uses the explicit phrase 賓主回互 (“guest and host 回互”), which supports reading 回互 here as a reciprocal switching of standpoints/roles rather than a fixed position. freely changing places.
As for those who take food by right livelihood, they must possess three kinds of falling. First is wearing fur and horns.
Second is not cutting off sounds and forms. Third is not receiving food. At that time Chou Bunna asked,
What fall is wearing fur and horns? The master said, It is the fall into kind. He asked,
What fall is not cutting off sounds and forms? The master said, It is the fall of following. He asked, Not receiving
food, what fall is that? The master said, It is the fall of nobility.

Because a monk asked about the essential formula of ruler and minister in the Five Ranks, the master said, The upright rank is precisely the realm of emptiness.
Originally there is not a single thing. The biased rank is precisely the realm of form, where the myriad appearances have shape. The biased within the upright is
turning away from principle and going toward phenomena. The upright within the biased is leaving phenomena and entering principle. Combined bearing is darkly responding to
all conditions, not falling into any forms of being, neither defiled nor pure, neither upright nor biased. Therefore it is called the empty
mysterious great way, the unattached true school. The former worthies from above esteemed this one rank as the most wondrous
and most profound. One should examine and distinguish it carefully. The ruler is the upright rank; the minister is the biased rank. Minister facing
the ruler is the upright within the biased. Ruler regarding the minister is the biased within the upright. The way of ruler and minister in accord
is speech of combined bearing. The monk asked, What is the ruler? The master said, Wondrous virtue honors the world-sphere,
lofty brightness illumines the great void. He asked, What is the minister? The master said, Numinous function broadens the sage's
way; true wisdom benefits all beings. He asked, What is minister facing ruler? The master said, Not falling
into the various divergent destinies, concentrating feeling and gazing on the sage's countenance. He asked, What is ruler regarding minister? The master said,
Though the wondrous countenance does not move, its light illumines and originally has no bias. He asked, What is the way of ruler and minister
in accord? The master said, Blended together, there is no inside or outside; harmoniously fused, above and below are level. The master also said,
In speaking of ruler and minister, biased and upright, one does not wish to violate the center. Therefore the minister addresses the ruler and does not dare
call him directly by name. So it is. This is the essential point of my Dharma lineage. Then he composed a verse, saying, Students must first
know their own school. Do not mix the true limit with blank emptiness. If the wondrous bright body is exhausted, know that contact wounds it.
The power lies in meeting conditions without borrowing the middle. When speaking, let it be such that fire cannot burn it; in hidden practice one must
be one with the ancients. No body yet affairs present transcends the branching roads; no affairs and no body falls into beginning
and end. He further composed verses on the Five Signs, saying, A white-robed one must bow to the minister; this matter is not
strange. For those whose families long wore caps and tassels, do not speak of the time when the nose falls away. Verse: At the zi hour one is in the
upright rank; the brightness is precisely in ruler and minister. Not yet apart from Tuṣita's realm, a black hen walks upon the snow.
Verse: In the flames, cold ice congeals; willow fluff flies in the ninth month. The clay ox bellows on the water's
surface; the wooden horse neighs chasing the wind. Verse: On the day of first descent in the royal palace, the jade rabbit could not
depart. Before attaining the meaning of no merit, why are humans and gods so late? Verse: Entirely blended,
it conceals principle and phenomena. The first trace suddenly leaves brightness. King Weiyin had not yet understood; how could Maitreya be awake
and clear?

When the master was traveling on pilgrimage, he asked Chan Master Guān of Wushi, What is the Vairocana teacher, the Dharma
body lord? Wushi said, If I were to tell you, then there would be something else. The master brought this up to
Dongshan. Dongshan said, A fine topic, it only lacks a follow-up phrase. Why not ask, Why
do you not say it? The master went back and put this question forward. Wushi said, If I say I do not
speak, that would make my mouth mute. If I say I do speak, that would make my tongue stammer. The master returned
and reported this to Dongshan. Dongshan deeply approved it.

Yunmen asked, What is the conduct of a śramaṇa? The master said, One who eats the monastery's crops
is one. Yunmen said, If one just goes on like that, then what? The master said, Can you even keep it
up? Yunmen said, I can. The master said, How do you keep it? Yunmen said,
Wearing clothes and eating rice, what is so hard about it? The master said, Why not say, Wearing fur and bearing horns?
Yunmen then bowed.

The master instructed the assembly, Everywhere they all cling to fixed rules. Why not give them a turning
word, so they can go free of doubt? Yunmen was in the assembly and stepped forward to ask, In the most secret place, why
is there no knowing there is? The master said, Precisely because it is so secret, therefore there is no knowing there is.
Yunmen said, How does one draw near to this person? The master said, Do not approach in the secretYunmen’s successor Xuedou separately said: Bodhidharma has come.
secret place. Yunmen said, If not in the secret secret place, then how? The master said,
Only then do you know how to approach. Yunmen said, Yes, yes.Miaoxi said: Muddy oil, and then a black lamp wick added.

Yunmen asked, If a person who does not change comes, will the master receive him or not? The master said, Caoshan
has no such idle time.

When Master Mi arrived, before they had met, Mi then sat down on the Chan seat. The master still
did not come out. Mi then left. The steward asked, The master's Chan seat, why was it
occupied by someone else? The master said, After leaving, he will come back again. Mi indeed returned and met
the master.

Zhiju came to consult and asked the master, The ancients upheld the person on the other side. How should a student
personally realize it fully? The master said, Step back and return to yourself; among ten thousand, not one is lost. At these words, Zhiju
suddenly forgot all abstruse understanding.

The master asked Jinfeng Zhi, What have you come to do? Jinfeng said, I came to roof a hut. The master
said, Is it finished yet? Jinfeng said, This side is finished. The master said, As for the other side,
what about that? Jinfeng said, I will report to the master when the workday is done. The master said, Just so,
just so.

Monk Qingshui asked, I am lonely and poor. I ask the master for relief. The master said, Reverend Shui,
come closer. Shui came closer. The master said, Three cups of wine at the Bai family in Quanzhou,
and after drinking them you still say your lips were not wet.Xuanjue said: In what place did they give him wine to drink.

Jingqing asked, As for the principle of clear emptiness, what is it when in the end there is no body? The master said, In principle
it is just so. But in actual fact, how is it? Jingqing said, As in principle, so in fact. The master said,
You may fool Caoshan alone, but what will you do about the eyes of the sages? Jingqing said, If
there were no eyes of the sages, how could one discern this not-so? The master said, In public office a needle is not allowed;
in private, carts and horses pass through.Dawei Zhe said: Although Caoshan was good at cutting, polishing, and refining, Jingqing’s jade was originally without flaw. If you want to understand, without a skilled hand it will in the end become a ruined vessel.

The master asked Senior De, A bodhisattva in samādhi hears a fragrant elephant crossing the river. From what
sutra does this come? The monk said, It comes from the Nirvāṇa Sutra. The master said, Did he hear before samādhi or after samādhi? The monk
said, master, you are drifting. The master said, You talk far too much; you have only spoken half of it.
The monk said, Then how about you, master? The master said, Catch it below the rapids.

The paper-robed Daoist came to consult. The master asked, Are you not the paper-robed Daoist? He said, I dare not.
The master said, What is the matter beneath the paper robe? The Daoist said, As soon as a single cloak is hung on
the body, the ten thousand dharmas are all thus. The master said, What is the function beneath the paper robe? The Daoist
stepped forward, answered, and immediately died standing. The master said, You only know how to go like this. Why not
know how to come like this? The Daoist suddenly opened his eyes and asked, The one numinous true nature, without relying on
womb and fetus, what is it then? The master said, That is not yet the marvelous. The Daoist said, What is
the marvelous? The master said, Not borrowing, and yet borrowing. The Daoist bade farewell and passed away. The master presented a verse and said:
The awakened nature, perfectly bright, is the formless body. Do not, through views and knowledge, vainly divide distant and near. When thought differs, then
the mysterious essence is obscured. When the mind deviates, it no longer neighbors the Way. Feeling divides the ten thousand dharmas, sinking into objects before it;
discriminating consciousness reflects in many ways and loses the original truth. If within these lines you wholly understand and meet it, then clearly
you are the person of old with nothing to do.

A monk cited Minister Lu Geng asking Nanquan, What is your surname? Nanquan said, Surname Wang.
Geng said, Does the king still have attendants or not? Nanquan said, The four ministers are not obscured. Geng
said, In what position does the king dwell? Nanquan said, Moss grows in the jade hall. He asked the master, The jade hall
overgrown with moss, what is its meaning? The master said, He does not dwell in the central position. The monk said, When the eight directions come
to court, what then? The master said, He does not accept their homage. The monk said, Then what is the use of coming to court?
The master said, Disobey, and they are beheaded. The monk said, Disobedience belongs to the ministers' side; I still do not know the ruler's intent,
what is it? The master said, The privy council has not received the command. The monk said, If so, then the work of ordering and regulating
all belongs entirely to the ministers and chancellors. The master said, Do you know the ruler's intent or not? The monk
said, One outside the court dares not discuss it. The master said, Just so, just so.

A monk asked, This student is sick all through his body. Please, master, heal me. The master said, I do not heal. The monk
said, Why do you not heal? The master said, To make it so you cannot seek life and cannot seek death
.

A monk asked the master, An ancient said, I have a great illness, not something the world can heal. I wonder, what
illness is this? The master said, The illness of not being able to gather it together. The monk said, Do all sentient
beings have this illness or not? The master said, Everyone has it completely. The monk said, Does the master
also have this illness or not? The master said, Right now I cannot even find where it begins. The monk said,
Why are all sentient beings not ill? The master said, If all sentient beings were ill,
then they would not be sentient beings. The monk said, I wonder whether the buddhas have this illness or not. The master
said, They do. The monk said, Since they have it, why are they not ill? The master said, Because they are lucid,
lucid.

A monk asked, Is not a śramaṇa one endowed with great compassion? The master said, Yes. The monk
said, If suddenly the six thieves come, then what? The master said, One must also have great compassion.
The monk said, How does one have great compassion? The master said, With one sword, sweep them all away. The monk said,
After sweeping them all away, then what? The master said, Only then is harmony attained.

A monk asked the master, Do the eyebrows and the eyes recognize each other or not? The master said, They do not recognize each other.
The monk said, Why do they not recognize each other? The master said, Because they are in the same place. The monk
said, Then in that case they are not distinct. The master said, Eyebrows are not eyes, and eyes
are not eyebrows. The monk said, What are eyes? The master said, Go straight to it. The monk
said, What are eyebrows? The master said, Caoshan instead has doubts. The monk said, master, why
do you instead doubt? The master said, If I did not doubt, I would go straight to it.

A monk asked, In the Five Positions, what is it like facing the guest? The master said, Which
position are you asking about right now? The monk said, I come from the partial position; I ask the master to receive me from the upright position.
The master said, I do not receive you. The monk said, Why do you not receive me? The master said, For fear of falling into the partial position
myself. The master then asked the monk, Tell me, this not receiving, is it facing the guest or not facing
the guest? The monk said, It was already facing the guest. The master said, Just so, just so.

A monk asked, From where are the ten thousand dharmas born? The master said, They are born from inversion. The monk said,
When there is no inversion, where are the ten thousand dharmas? The master said, They are there. The monk said, Where
are they? The master said, What are you doing upside down?

A monk asked, Why can the unmouting grass hide a fragrant elephant? The master said, Reverend,
luckily you are a capable man. Why ask Caoshan again?

A monk asked, The three realms are in turmoil, the six destinies are dark and dim; how does one distinguish forms? The master said,
Do not distinguish forms. The monk said, Why not distinguish forms? The master said, If you distinguish forms, then
you are deluded.

The master heard the bell sound and then said, Ah, ah. A monk asked, master, what are you
doing? The master said, It struck my heart. The monk had no reply.Wuzu Jie said: A thief has a guilty heart.

The master asked the attendant, Where have you come from? He said, I was dragging the vinegar tub away and back. The master said, If you come to
a dangerous place, then how will you drag it? The attendant had no reply.Yunju, speaking in place, said: Just right to apply effort. Shushan, speaking in place, said: Earnestly
you must let go, only then will it do.

The master. One day he entered the monks’ hall and warmed himself by the fire. A monk said, Today is very cold. The master
said, You must know there is one who is not cold. The monk said, Who is the one not cold. The master poked the fire
and showed him. The monk said, Do not say no one is any good. The master threw down the poker. The monk said, I
have reached this point and still do not understand. The master said, The sun shines on the cold pool, bright and ever brighter.

A monk asked, The one who does not take the myriad dharmas as companions, what kind of person is that. The master said, You
tell me, with so many people in Hongzhou city, where do they go.

A monk asked, What is the sword without a blade. The master said, It is not made by tempering and forging. The monk said,
What about one who uses it. The master said, Whoever meets it loses his life. The monk said, What about one who does not meet it
then. The master said, His head too must fall. The monk said, It is true enough that those who meet it all perish.
But why does the head fall for one who does not meet it. The master said, Have you not heard it said, It can exhaust all things.
The monk said, What is it like after all is exhausted. The master said, Only then do you know this sword is there.

A monk asked, Within appearances, what is true. The master said, Appearance itself is truth. The monk said, By what
is it shown. The master lifted up the tray.

A monk asked, In illusion itself, what is true. The master said, Illusion itself is originally true. The monk said,Fayan separately said: Illusion is originally not real.
Through illusion, how is it revealed. The master said, Illusion itself is the revealing. The monk said, If so,Fayan separately said: Illusion is precisely without substance.
then from beginning to end one never leaves illusion. The master said, Seek the mark of illusion and it cannot be found.

A monk asked, I will not ask about mind is Buddha. What is neither mind nor Buddha. The master
said, Rabbit horns need not be called nonexistent. Ox horns need not be called existent.

Asked, What is the person who is always present. The master said, You happen to meet Caoshan stepping out for a moment. He said,
What is the person who is always not present. The master said, Hard to come by.

A monk asked, Is intending not a kind. The master said, Even not intending is still a kind. The monk
said, What is the different. The master said, Can you fail to know pain and itch.

Someone asked, The ancients said everyone fully has it. I am buried in dust and confusion. Do I still have it
or not. The master said, Hold out your hand. Then he counted on his fingers and said, One two three four five
six, enough.
A monk asked, When Master Luzu faced the wall, what was he indicating. The master covered his ears with his hands.

A monk asked, I have heard the ancients say, No one falls to the ground without relying on the ground to rise.
What is falling. The master said, Consent is it. The monk said, What is rising. The master
said, Rising.

A monk asked, When the son returns to the father, why does the father pay no heed at all. The master said, In principle it should be
so. The monk said, Then where is the grace between father and son. The master said, Only then is the grace of father and son
fulfilled. The monk said, What is the grace of father and son. The master said, Knives and axes cannot split it.

Asked, What is it like when the spiritual robe is not hung up. The master said, Caoshan, filial duty fulfilled. He said, After filial duty is fulfilled
what then. The master said, Caoshan loves toppled wine.

Asked, The teaching says, The great ocean does not lodge a corpse. What is the great ocean.
The master said, It contains the ten thousand things. The monk said, Why does it not lodge a corpse. The master
said, One without breath cannot attach. The master said, The ten thousand things are not its merit. Breath cut off
has its own condition. The monk said, Above this, is there still any matter or not. The master said, To say there is or to say there is not
is acceptable, but what can you do when the Dragon King presses his sword.

Asked, With what understanding can one skillfully answer public challenges. The master said, By not presenting
phrases. The monk said, Then what is there to challenge. The master said, Knives and axes cannot cut in. The monk
said, In such questioning and challenging, is there anyone who does not assent. The master said, Yes. The monk
said, Who is that person. The master said, Caoshan.

A monk asked, What thing in the world is most precious. The master said, A dead cat's head is most precious.
The monk said, Why is a dead cat's head most precious. The master said, No one can set a price on it.

A monk asked, How is wordlessness revealed. The master said, Do not reveal it here. The monk said,
Where then is it revealed. The master said, Last night by the bedside, I lost three coins.

A monk asked, What is it like before the sun comes out. The master said, Caoshan too once came like that.
The monk said, What about after it comes out. The master said, Still half a month's journey short of Caoshan.

The master asked a monk, What are you doing. The monk said, Sweeping the ground. The master said, Sweep before the Buddha. Sweep behind the Buddha.
The monk said, I sweep front and back at one time. The master said, Bring Caoshan his sandals to pass over to him
here.Wuzu Jie, speaking for the monk, said: Master, what kind of mind-practice is this.

A monk asked, I carry uncut jade and cast myself before the teacher. Please carve and polish me. The master said, I do not carve or polish. The monk
said, Why do you not carve or polish. The master said, You must know Caoshan has good hands.

A monk asked, What are Caoshan's household people like. The master said, White hair worn all over the head, and on top
a single flower.

A monk asked, An ancient worthy said, In the whole great earth there is only this person. I ask, what kind of
person is that. The master said, There must not be a second moon. The monk said, What is the second
moon. The master said, Even so, elder brother, you must settle it yourself. The monk said, Then what is the first
moon. The master said, Dangerous.

A monk asked, Through the twelve hours of the day, how should a student preserve and sustain it. The master said, As if passing through a land of poison magic,
not a single drop of water may touch you.

A monk asked, Who is the lord of the dharma body. The master said, To say Qin has no people. The monk said,
Is this then it. The master said, Cut him down.

A monk asked, By being intimate with what companion of the Way does one constantly hear what has never been heard. The master said, Sharing together
one quilt. The monk said, That is still the master's hearing. What is constantly hearing
what has never been heard. The master said, It is not the same as wood or stone. The monk said, Which is first.
Which is after. The master said, Have you not heard it said, Constantly hearing what has never been heard.

A monk asked, In the realm, who is the one pressing the sword. The master said, Caoshan. The monk said, Whom does he intend to kill. The master said, If there is anything at all, he kills it all. The monkFadeng separately said: You are not like this.
person.
said, If he suddenly meets his own father and mother, then what. The master said, What is there to pick and choose. The monk
said, Then what about oneself. The master said, Who can do anything to me. The monk said, Why not
kill yourself. The master said, There is no place to start.

A monk asked, When a poor household is robbed, what is it like. The master said, It cannot be taken all away. The monk
said, Why can it not be taken all away. The master said, The thief is one of the family.

Asked, When one ox drinks water and five horses do not neigh, what is it like. The master said, Caoshan knows
how to avoid speaking. He also said separately, Caoshan, filial duty fulfilled.

A monk asked, The one who is always sinking in the sea of birth and death, what kind of person is that. The master said,
The second moon. The monk said, Does he still seek to get out. The master said, He also seeks release.
It is just that there is no road. The monk said, When he gets free, what kind of person can receive him. The master said,
One carrying an iron cangue.

A monk asked, Snow covers a thousand mountains. Why is the solitary peak not white. The master said, You must know
there is a difference within difference. The monk said, What is difference within difference. The master said, Not falling into the colors of
the various mountains.

A monk cited this. Yaoshan asked a monk, How old are you. He said, Seventy two. Yaoshan said, Is it
really seventy two. He said, Yes. Yaoshan then struck him. What is the meaning of this. The master said,
The first arrow was still passable. The later arrow shot the man deep. The monk said, How can one avoid this
blow. The master said, Once the king's decree is carried out, the feudal lords clear the road.

A monk asked Xiangyan, What is the Way. Xiangyan said, A dragon chanting within a dead tree. The monk
said, What is a person in the Way. Xiangyan said, Eyes in a skull. The monk did not
understand, so he asked Shishuang, What is a dragon chanting within a dead tree. Shishuang said, It still
carries joy. The monk said, What are eyes in a skull. Shishuang said, It still
carries consciousness. Again he did not understand, so he brought it up to the master. The master said, Old Shishuang the śrāvaka
makes this kind of interpretation. Then he gave a verse, Dragon chant in dead wood truly sees the Way.
In the skull, when consciousness is gone, the eyes first become bright. When joy and consciousness are exhausted, all trace is exhausted. How can the person himself
distinguish purity within the turbid. The monk then again asked the master, What is a dragon chanting within a dead tree.
The master said, The bloodline is not cut off. He said, What are eyes in a skull. The master
said, It does not dry out completely. He said, I wonder, is there still anyone who can hear it. The master said, In the whole
great earth, there is not one person who does not hear it. He said, I wonder, the dragon chant in dead wood,
what line or phrase is it. The master said, I do not know what line or phrase it is. Those who hear it all
perish.
A monk asked, What is the great meaning of the Buddha-Dharma? The master said, Filling ditches and plugging ravines.

A monk asked, What is the lion? The master said, The various beasts cannot approach it. The monk said,
What is the lion cub? The master said, One that can swallow its father and mother. The monk said, Since it is that
the various beasts cannot approach, why then is it swallowed by its cub? The master said, Have you not seen it said:
If the child roars, both ancestors and father are all ended. The monk said, What about after they are ended? The master
said, The whole body returns to the father. The monk said, When the ancestors are ended, where does the father return?
The master said, That place too is ended. The monk said, Why then did you just now say the whole body returns to the father? The master
said, It is like a prince who can accomplish the affairs of a whole state. He also said, Venerable, in this matter
you must not remain isolated and stuck. You must scatter a few blossoms even on a dead tree.
A monk asked, As soon as right and wrong arise and the mind is thrown into confusion, what then? The master said, Cut, cut.

The master, after reading the Dharmakaya verse composed by Dushun and the Layman Fu, said, I do not wish
to put it that way. The disciples asked him to compose another. After composing a verse, he also annotated
it. The wording was: He originally is not me; I originally am not he. If heNot me.Not him.
has no me, he dies; if I have no him, I remain. If he is like me, he is Buddha; if I am like him, I am a donkey. If one does not eat the Empty King s stipend, what need is thereI rely on you to bring it to life.There is nothing separate.Yet still,
it is not Buddha.Neither of the two is established.If you encounter imperial food, you must spit it right out.
for a wild goose to carry a letter? I sing with my body turned crosswise; you look at the hair on my back. It may seem like the White Snow song, yet I still fear it is only a Ba song.No trust in the letter.It is proclaimed for the sake of faith.It does not match with you.
resemble.One might have thought it was white snow.In the transmitted text this line has no annotation.

A monk asked, When the bright moon hangs in the sky, what then? The master said, Still a fellow below the steps. The monk
said, Please, Master, receive me up the steps. The master said, Come see me after the moon has set.The Zhaozhou Recorded Sayings are the same here, and now both are
preserved.

The master addressed the assembly, There is a man who, from the top of a ten-thousand-zhang cliff, leaps straight down.
What sort of person is this? The assembly had no answer. Daoyan stepped out and said, Not existing. The master said,
Not existing what? Yan said, Only then can he fall and not shatter. The master deeply approved him.

A monk cited this: One day Xiyuan was heating bath water himself. A monk asked, Why not have a novice do it?
Xiyuan clapped his hands three times. The monk asked the master about it. The master said, Clapping and striking the palms are of one kind,
but within that Xiyuan is extraordinary and strange. As for Judi s one-finger Chan, it is because at the point of undertaking it
it was not fully exact. The monk then asked the master, Xiyuan clapping his hands, is that not a matter on the level of slave boys and serving girls?
The master said, It is. The monk said, Is there still something beyond that? The master said, There is.
The monk said, What is the matter beyond? The master shouted, You slave boy and serving girl!

The commander of Nanzhou, King Zhong of Nanping, had long heard that the master possessed the Way. He offered every courtesy to invite him, but the master did not
go. He only wrote a verse and gave it to the envoy, saying: A battered dead tree leans against the cold forest; how many times
it has met spring, yet its heart has not changed. Even woodcutters, seeing it, do not gather it. Why should the men of Ying so bitterly
search for it?Mr. Zhong invited him but he did not rise. He only wrote the Damei mountain-dwelling verse in reply.

The master composed a verse on the Four Prohibitions, saying: Do not travel the road where mind operates. Do not hang on the original robe. Why
must it be exactly thus? Most of all avoid the time before arising.

He showed a verse to students, saying: To realize through conditions brings quick accord. To settle and dissolve in the body-substance gains
power slowly. A sudden flash reveals the original has no location. My teacher only briefly spoke the inconceivable.

He addressed the assembly, For those in the sangha, beneath this robe and seam, it is necessary in principle to understand through to the matter beyond.
Do not treat it lightly. If, at the point of undertaking, it is clear, then you can turn all the sages
behind your own back, and only then gain freedom. If you cannot turn them, even if you have learned
to full completion, you must still stand behind them with folded hands. What big talk can you make? If
you can turn yourself, then when all coarse and heavy situations come, you can be master of them all. Suppose
you fall to the ground in the mud; even then you can be master. For example, a monk asked Yaoshan,
In the teachings of the Three Vehicles, is there still the Patriarch s meaning or not? He answered, There is. The monk said, Since there is,
why then did Bodhidharma come? Yaoshan said, He came precisely because there is. Is this not
being master and turning it back to oneself? As the sutra says, Great Penetrating Wisdom Victory Buddha
sat at the seat of enlightenment for ten kalpas. The Buddha-Dharma did not appear before him, and he did not attain Buddhahood.
Kalpa here means obstruction. This is called tenfold completion. It is also called cutting off leakages. It is only that
the ten directions are cut off. Yet if one does not forget the great fruit, then this guarding and clinging is called
casual undertaking, with no distinction of noble or base. I often see monasteries fond of discussing one kind
and two kinds. Can they actually establish the matter? These are only words about what goes onward,
a layout of the road. Have you not seen Nansen say: Even if you have tenfold completion, you still fall short of Master Wang
by a single thread. This is indeed a difficult matter. At this point you must be very careful before clarity
and ease can be attained. Whether heaven, hell, hungry ghosts, or beasts, in every place
do not shift or change. Originally you are the same old person, only you do not walk the old road. If there is
delighting mind, it again becomes attachment. If you can get free, what is there to choose? An ancient worthy said, Only
fearing one may not obtain rebirth. Tell me, how is it? People nowadays, speaking of some pure
and clean place, love to talk of what goes onward. This sickness is the hardest to cure. As for worldly coarse
and heavy matters, those are actually light. The sickness of purity and cleanliness is heavy. Even Buddha-flavor and Patriarch-flavor are all
attachments. My former teacher said, To stir the mind is breaking precepts. If you get a taste, that is breaking
the fast. But what do you call taste? It is only Buddha-flavor and Patriarch-flavor. As soon as delighting mind appears,
that is breaking precepts. If we speak now of breaking the fast and breaking precepts, then right now at the time of the three karmas
of ordination, they are already broken. As for coarse greed, anger, and ignorance, though hard to cut off, they are still
light. But if it is non-action, no affairs, purity and cleanliness, this is heavy beyond anything more.
The Patriarchs came into the world only for this. It was not only for you. At present do not
take it lightly. A black servant and a white ox practice faster. It is not because they have Chan or the Way.
As for your various pursuits, seeking Buddha, seeking Patriarch, and even bodhi and nirvana, when
will they ever rest and be accomplished? All of it is mind of arising and ceasing. Therefore they are not equal to the black servant
and the white ox, dull and without knowing, not knowing Buddha, not knowing Patriarch, not even bodhi and nir-
vana, nor good and evil, cause and effect. They only eat grass when hungry and drink water when thirsty. If
you can be like this, do not worry that it will not be accomplished. Have you not seen it said: Calculation does not succeed; therefore
one must know it is there. Then one can wear fur and horns, pull the plow and drag it along. Gaining this advantage, one only then
comes a little closer. Have you not seen Maitreya, Akshobhya, and all the wondrous joyful worlds, which are by those
who are beyond called shameless, lazy bodhisattvas? They are also called transformation-
birth-and-death. That is still feared to be only small laziness. In the fundamental matter, how should it be? You very much
must be careful before it can be so. Everyone has a ground of the sitting cloth. Even if a Buddha appears in the world, he cannot encroach on it.
Practice and realize in this way; do not chase quick gain. If you want to know this matter, even if
you are made into a Buddha or a Patriarch, it is only this. And if you fall into the three evil paths, hell, or the six
destinies, it is only this. Although it seems of no use, still you cannot leave it.
You must become its master. If you can be master, then there is no transformation
or change. If you cannot be master, then it is transformation and change. Have you not seen Yongjia say,
Vast and heedless, one invites calamity. A monk asked, What is vast and heedless inviting calamity?
He said, This whole thing is exactly it. The monk asked, How can one avoid it? He said, Knowing it is there is enough.
What use is avoidance? Bodhi, nirvana, affliction, ignorance, and the like are all
not to be avoided. Even worldly coarse and heavy matters, just know they are there and that is enough.
Do not avoid them. To avoid is to go along with transformation and change. Even becoming Buddha or Patriarch, bodhi
and nirvana, these calamities are by no means small. Why is it thus? Only because of
transformation and change. If there is no transformation and change, then one must be free at every point of contact.

On a summer night in the xin-you year of Tianfu, the master asked the monastery steward, What is today s date?
He replied, The fifteenth day of the sixth month. The master said, All my life, wherever Caoshan traveled,
I always took ninety days as one summer retreat. Tomorrow at the chen hour, I will go traveling.
When the time came, he burned incense, sat upright at ease, and passed away. He had lived sixty-two years and had been ordained thirty-seven years.
They buried his whole body on the western slope of the mountain. He was posthumously titled Chan Master Yuanzheng, and his stupa was named Fuyuan
。End.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Caoshan Benji of Fuzhou, Lower Scroll

cb:div.byline: Compiled and arranged by the Japanese monk Genkei
cb:div.p:
Ascending the hall, a monk asked, What is a great icchantika person? The master said, One who does not fear karma. The monk
said, What is a person of ignorance? The master said, From beginning to end, not awakened. The monk said,
Which of these two is prior? The master said, The ignorant one. The monk said, Why is the icchantika person
behind? The master said, The one who goes on. The monk said, Then in that case, the ignorant one
does not go on from today. The master said, Yes. The monk said, Since he does not go on from today,
from where does ignorance come? The master said, It dares not enter the place of light. The monk said, Is that not
neither bright nor dark? The master said, It is. The monk said, At just such a time, what then? The master
He said, It does not receive contact. The master continued, Icchantikas are of many kinds. One kind is this:
killing father, killing mother, drawing the Buddha's blood, breaking up the harmonious sangha, and destroying monasteries.
Such ones are certainly bound to receive direct retribution and undergo many sufferings. Another kind also does as above,
but here father means ignorance and mother means craving. They do not believe there is Buddha, Dharma, or Sangha to be
broken, nor monasteries to be destroyed. They take all this as attained by karmic mind, and so they fall and also receive various
false karmic fruits and retributions. As before, their rise and fall differ. Another kind knows there is one's own
original matter, and calls that father and mother. It is not obtained from outside, with no cultivation and no realization, neither
cause nor effect, not received from a teacher, not gained through realization and practice. Not raising the view of father
is called killing, and not raising the view of mother is called harming. This is all one's fundamental affair, not
grasping and not preserving, therefore called killing and harming. If there is the slightest trace of cherishing or weighting it, savor cannot be realized.
This is knowing there is one's own matter. Therefore it is called the great icchantika. With this one sets the subtle power in motion.
This is precisely to embody and take responsibility for the family affair realized in the ancestral vehicle from above, to cut off the dark path and break through
all crooked byways, just as the old man of Xinfeng obscurely indicated.

National Teacher Zhong suddenly called the attendant. The attendant came and stood there. The National Teacher lowered his head. The attendant
stood a long while and went out. The National Teacher called the attendant again. After doing this three times, he said,
I had thought I was letting you down, but instead you are letting me down. Baizhang cited this and asked Zhaozhou, The National
Teacher calling the attendant three times, what was his meaning. Zhaozhou said, It is like a man writing characters in the dark.
Though the characters are not formed, the patterning is already there. Later someone again cited it and asked the master, The National Teacher
calling the attendant three times, what was his meaning. The master said, When the attendant came back the second time,
he said, I do not believe the master was calling me.

Nanquan said, Before one has a formed embryo, is there speech or not. Someone cited this and asked
Xuefeng. Xuefeng said, Say there is or say there is not, and you get thirty blows. Then he asked Zhaoqing,
and Qing said, Let him speak for himself. He also cited it and asked the master. The master said, There is. He said, Please,
Master, give a side glance. The master said, With what thing do you hear. He said, Can the deaf still hear or not.
The master said, If the deaf could hear, then they would possess ears and eyes. He said, What person can hear.
The master said, One not yet formed in the womb.

A monk asked the master, The teachings say a single phrase can swallow hundreds of thousands of meanings. What is that
one phrase. The master said, Even a needle cannot pierce it.

There was a lecture master taking leave of Nanquan. Quan asked, Where are you going. He replied, Down the mountain.
Quan said, First of all, do not slander Teacher Wang. He replied, How would I dare slander
the master. Quan spat out water and said, How much. The lecture master then went out. The master said, Good thing
indeed.

One day Guishan called the head monk. The head monk came. Shan said, I called the head monk. Why did you
come. The head monk had no reply. The master answered on his behalf, I also know the master was not calling
me. He then had the attendant call the first seat. The first seat came. Shan said, I
called the first seat. Why did you come. The master answered on his behalf, If he had the attendant call,
I feared he would not come.

A monk took leave of Yaoshan to return home. Yaoshan asked, There is a man, his whole body red and raw,
lying among thorns. The monk said, If so, then this student will not go home.
Yaoshan said, Just know to go home, and I will give you the method of fasting. He asked, What is the fasting
method. Shan said, Each day when ascending the hall, do not bite through a single grain of rice. The master said,
As for the ancient worthy who said the man with the whole body red and raw is simply the ugly
man, no one can approach him, with no place to take hold. And further he said he lies among thorns.
This only speaks of present daily use. There too there is no place to make a handle of it, nor of guarding
and preserving it as a matter of maintenance. At that time a monk asked the master, What about when the whole body is red and raw.
The master said, Carry it. He said, Carry whom. The master said, Do not let the redness and rawness reach you,
Reverend. He again asked, Between the ugly man and the man all over red and raw, which one is
weightier. The master said, The greatly ugly man is weightier. The master also brought this up and asked a monk, The greatly maintaining
man, what does he maintain. He answered for him, All day it is behind his back; he has never
once glanced at it.

Whenever master Juzhi was questioned, he only raised one finger. Later there was a novice who, when
an outsider asked, What essential teaching does the master preach, likewise held up one finger. Zhi
heard of it and thereupon cut off his finger with a blade. The novice, bearing the pain, cried out and ran away. Zhi then
called him back. The novice turned his head. Zhi again raised his finger, and the novice suddenly understood.
As Zhi was about to pass away, he told the assembly, I obtained Tianlong's one-finger Zen. In a whole lifetime
I could not exhaust its use. Having said this, he passed in silence. The master said, Where Juzhi took it up was crude and rough. He only
recognized one function and one circumstance.

Dongshan ascended the hall and said, The Way without mind accords with man; man without mind accords with the Way. If you wish to know the meaning within this,
one is old and one is not old. Later a monk cited this and asked the master, What is the old one.
The master said, Not propped up. He said, What is the not old one. The master said, A dead tree.

A monk asked the master, Vimalakirti remained silent and Manjushri praised it as excellent. I wonder whether that truly matched Vimalakirti's
intent. The master said, Can you bind empty space. The monk said, If so, then
it did not match Vimalakirti's intent. The master said, And why would he agree to that. The monk said, In the end, is there
any place of return. The master said, If there is a place of return, then it is the same as those two gentlemen. The monk said,
then how about master. The master said, Wait until you suffer Vimalakirti's illness, then it can be discussed.

Dongshan came to Bishu. Bi asked, What have you come for. Shan said, To draw near the master.
Bi said, If it is truly drawing near, what use is there in moving two pieces of skin. Shan had no
reply. The master said, One son personally got it. A monk asked Dongshan, Among the three bodies, which
body does not fall into enumeration. Shan said, Here I am always keenly pressed. A monk asked the master, Formerly the teacher
said, Here I am always keenly pressed. What was his meaning. The master said, If you want a head, then chop it off
at once.

As Dongshan was about to enter final stillness, he told the assembly, I have an idle name in the world. Who can
remove it for me. The assembly had no reply. Then a novice stepped forward and said, Please, master, your Dharma name.
Shan said, My idle name has already fallen away. The master said, From ancient times until now, no one has discerned
it.

While Wuzhu was drinking tea, Manjushri picked up a glass cup and asked, Is there still this in the South
or not. Zhu said, No. Manjushri said, What do you ordinarily use to drink tea. Zhu
had no reply. The master answered on his behalf, For a long time I have received the Great Being pressing his sword; why is he dwelling in a mote of dust
at all.

The master said, Before Dipankara there are two kinds. One does not yet know there is, like milk mixed with blood.
One knows there is, as if obtaining the original thing before intention has sprouted. This is called before Dipankara.
One kind of knowing there is, amid coming and going, words and speech, sounds and forms, right and wrong, still does not belong to
correct illuminating function, and likewise does not receive prediction. It is like milk mixed with blood, an affair on the side of leakage and loss.
This is called after Dipankara. It is simply that the affairs of the three times are exhausted, inner and outer feelings forgotten, and one attains no
interruption. Only then is it rightly named Dipankara, and thus said to have received prediction.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Explanation of Dongshan's Manifest Key to the Five Positions
cb:div.head: Explanation of Dongshan's Manifest Key to the Five Positions
cb:div.p:
As for what the late master clarified regarding bias and upright, and the combined carrying, and the like, the intent was that of the late master himself. It was not to explain ranks of merit and progressive cultivation, nor to mix in scriptural phrases. It is simply a profound discussion outside the standard pattern, aiming at the most subtle meaning. It only clarifies the original substance handed down from above,
present before us, silently tallying with the way of the ancient sages. Now I see various scholars interpreting and selecting the late master’s intent, and it seems they have displayed it wrongly. I cannot avoid briefly describing their errors. I should be ashamed if I mix up its functions. Among these there are cases of borrowing a rank to clarify
merit, and borrowing merit to clarify a rank. The threads of conditions are many. The function lies in the moment at hand. One must watch the momentum with which the words come, not fail the arriving opportunity. The subtlety lies only in fitting grace.。

From the upright position one turns to the partial. Distinguishing it within the partial, this completes both meanings.When the upright rank turns toward the biased, it is because it does not confront things. Though it does not confront
things, still it fully possesses them. In the upright, no function is called biased; full function is called complete. These are two meanings. If asked what full means, it is said: a person who attains without looking back. This is the upright rank not made explicit as coming forth. If a Buddha appears in the world, it is just thus;
if a Buddha does not appear in the world, it is also just thus. Therefore thousands of sages and myriads of sages all return to bearing responsibility in the upright rank. In the upright with bias, this rank is still present. First of all, it must not be stirred. If scholars select the solitary release outside things,
arising before the many sages, and say this is the upright rank yet complete, in truth they have bent the upright rank. This type of saying is where the ancients spoke and traces still remain; it still has not reached no words within words. This is instead called not the upright rank,
because there are words within the words. This may be called a flawed combined carrying. It must not be called combined carrying coming forth.

Though the partial position is partial, it also completes both meanings. Distinguished within conditions, this is no speech within speech.
no speech.For at the point of function nothing is fixed. If nothing is fixed, then the true is not a constant function. As for the biased rank being biased yet also complete, in function there is nothing it does not touch. These are two meanings. Though clarified within function, in the words it does not
wound anything. This is talking all day as if not talking at all. It is also said: the biased rank yet complete, and it also possesses not touching within conditions.

Or there are those who come from within the upright position; this is speech within no speech.Coming within the upright does not combine with conditions. As when Yaoshan
said, I have a phrase I have never spoken to anyone. Daowu said, It comes along together. This was his subtle understanding. As in the saying to the Hunan Inspector, there are many such examples. In such matters one must bring them out properly and not confuse higher and
lower. This is called words within no words. Again, I have a phrase I have never spoken to anyone. In such question and answer, it must come out fittingly and not at odd angles. If it goes askew, then one does not know what is there. Every phrase is no words, and does not
establish exaltedness, and does not fall to either side. Therefore it is called coming within the upright. Coming in the upright rank clarifies that the upright rank does not involve conditions. Further examples are cited, such as: before the black beans sprout, how is it. Or: there is one person
with no outgoing or incoming breath. Or: before having a formed womb, are there words or not. And: the place from which the Buddhas of the ten directions emerge. Such examples are called words within no words. There are also cases of borrowing an occasion. As for coming within the upright rank,
in this rank, the one answering must clarify its embodied thingness within the biased rank. One must not clarify it by entering the upright rank. This point must be understood. For example, the late master asked a Silla monk, Before crossing the sea, where were you. There was no
reply. He himself answered in place: Right now, having crossed the sea, where are you. Or as when the late master spoke for Elder Shenwei in the staff matter, saying: Now that it is brought out, is there anyone who can discern it. Though in such examples it is recognized within conditions,
it is not the same as going onward. If one cannot distinguish this, I fear later people will gather it into merit and take it for the matter above. For example, scholars classify the question, What was the patriarch’s meaning, and the answer, Wait until a gelded ox gives birth and then I will tell you, and say this is
coming within the upright rank. This kind of saying absolutely must not be called coming within the upright rank. It may be called a question and answer on the road of dark learning, both so, yet it is a different path. It also must not be called combined carrying, because it is too explicit. Even if
host and guest alternate and interpenetrate, it can only be called flawed combined carrying.

Or there are those who come from within the partial position; this is no speech within speech.Coming within the biased rank then combines with conditions. As when it is said:
What should this right now be called for it to be right. No reply. The late master answered in place himself: No, no. There are many such examples. This is called no words within words. Speech comes from the sounds and forms of the four elements, yet does not establish
location, right or wrong. Therefore, if it is discerned within conditions, it is called coming within the biased rank. Examples include: What thing comes thus. Also: when both light and object are forgotten, what thing is it then. Also: cultivate concentration and wisdom equally
to clearly see Buddha nature. There are many such examples. They are called no words within words. Coming within the biased rank clarifies the substance by means of things, as in What thing comes thus, and when both light and object are forgotten, what thing is it then. This
kind of saying uses merit to clarify the rank, and is also an old example I gave. What thing comes thus. Though this example is recognized within conditions, it is not the same as going onward. Again, cultivate concentration and wisdom equally to clearly see Buddha nature. As for this principle,
how is it. This too was an example I first raised. Again, both light and object forgotten is a rule from the teachings; it is not the same as dark learning. One only wants, from within those doctrinal rules, to bring out what in the school is a matter outside dark learning. Only
as in, the outgoing breath does not rely on the many conditions, the incoming breath does not dwell in the aggregates and realms. This saying is entirely about merit and is not the same as recognition within conditions. It too is an old example I gave. The host side draws it into the upright rank, saying there is one person with no out-
going or incoming breath, making him know there is an upright rank. There is also the pure and clean rank at the extreme of carried merit, which can also be called coming within the biased rank. This is hard to distinguish and must be sorted out. For example, scholars classify a monk asking the late master, What
is the dark meaning. The master said: Like the tongue of a dead man. He also asked: Through the twelve hours, what should one offer. He said: Nothing. They say this is coming within the biased rank. These two examples must not be called coming within the biased rank. Each must be
distinguished separately. As for the dark meaning example, it may be grouped with merit. These two examples together must not be called the biased rank nor combined carrying. This has already been clearly refuted earlier. They are borrowing merit to clarify rank, and borrowing rank to clarify merit.
It is the same as this.

Or there are those who come with mutual integration. Here one does not speak of speech or no speech. Here
one must go straight on from the front. Here one cannot but turn round and complete it. The matter must turn round completely.
。Combined carrying coming forth means the force of the words is neither biased nor upright, neither existent nor nonexistent, like complete yet not complete, like lacking yet not lacking. One only gets the direct face and goes on. If it goes on, then nothing is fixed. If nothing is fixed, then the utmost subtle
speech is not a matter rounded out by ordinary feeling. For example, the late master speaking in place in the Wenshu tea-drinking saying said: Borrow this and see, can you. Also like Cuiwei saying: What do you eat every day.

Yet words spoken along the way are altogether sickness. As for the person concerned, he must first discern words and phrases.
Go straight on from the front. Speech comes in this way; no speech goes in this way. In a true adept's house
there is not an absence of words, yet it does not involve speech or no speech. This is called integrated
speech. Integrated speech is wholly without fixed marks.Combined carrying coming forth does not fall into words or no words, as in Yaoshan’s belt-knife
saying. This is combined-carrying speech. At the moment one must watch the momentum of the words as they come. Sometimes it goes straight ahead face to face, sometimes there is emptiness within difference. If this is not subtly understood, one will be off by a thousand or ten thousand miles. Examples of combined carrying coming forth include
the Wenshu tea-drinking saying, and This person now, where has he gone. Yunyan said: How, how. Also: Right now, how is it. There are many such examples. There is also combined carrying within merit, which seems like
the matter above. It must be discerned on the spot. If it falls into a pure and subtle place, one must know there is still a matter there. If it should go, then go; if it should stop, then stop. In all the many turnings one must not be crude. As for the momentum of speech in questioning and answering on both sides,
in their mutual response, none goes outside the five ranks. It is only that some speech is coarse or fine, and some answers shallow or deep. Therefore the late master, within what is not verbal phrases, forced it into words, all for responding to conditions and setting forth these essentials. For example, a person of great ignorance
is the whole body, unlike an icchantika. An icchantika knows there is something, yet covers it over; though covered over, it still becomes filial support. To cover over means not preserving the patriarchs and Buddhas and one’s own original father and mother. The person red and rotting is one who does not
return to the whole and bear it fully, and does not set up the supreme. The person of great preserving and maintaining is one whose feet are pricked and enter the mud, not someone of petty maintenance. As for combined carrying coming forth, it must be like the Wenshu tea-drinking saying, and the late master’s answer to Yun-
yan’s ginger-hoeing saying, and Master An’s Dharma hall saying, and Yaoshan Chun the patched-robe monk washing Buddha saying. Among these, the most subtle combined carrying is none beyond Yaoshan’s answer to Daowu’s belt-knife saying, and Baizhang descending from the hall when the assembly was about to
disperse but had not yet dispersed, and someone asked: What is this. Yaoshan, hearing these words from afar, said: Here it is. Then one can speak of hidden-side combined carrying, using merit to clarify the thing, using the thing to clarify merit, using excess to clarify merit, using merit to clarify excess, and so on.
As for the sayings put forth by Yaoshan, Xinfeng, and the various earlier worthies, they surpass and enter the upright rank. These are already marvelous and extraordinary phrases of profound discourse. Next, when it comes to those of only slight attainment, they are drawn into the upright rank. This type of saying is commonly used.
As for me, because the affairs of abbacy are many, I cannot be detailed, and have only briefly clarified a small portion. All of you people should not lightly slight this. If there are further hesitations and blockages, they should be resolved at once. You must exert yourselves in practice.
Make this matter unbroken into the endless future. Do not grow lax and let it leak away. If you encounter one who is pure and simple, that is a rare vessel, and it must not be concealed.

When Elder Taizhi was nearing death, he said to people, Yunyan did not know there is. I
regret that at the time I did not tell him. Even so, it still does not go against Yaoshan.
Master Cai said, Look at how Elder Zhi acts like such an old grandmother. Nanquan said:
Moving among the different kinds. And Master Mi does not yet know it.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Annotated Explanation of Dongshan's Verses on the Five Ranks
cb:div.head: Annotated Explanation of Dongshan's Verses on the Five Ranks
cb:div.p:
The Biased within the Correct: at the third watch, before the moon is bright in the first part of night. Do not blame them for meeting and not recognizing one another; dimly they still harbor old-time misgivings.Nodding in the dark.Discern it before black and white have met, when the sprout has not yet arisen, just
what time is it right now. Here there is no sun or moon. It does not speak of before or after.Forgotten too. And then. How again.
It comes in contradiction through kalpas. If so, then both withdraw with folded hands.These two lines are one meaning, yet in the end they are not alike.
It is also said: complete. Also: what is weighty today. Also: if so, then one cannot deceive oneself.

The Correct within the Biased: losing dawn, the old woman meets an ancient mirror. Clearly face to face, there is no further real thing, yet in confusion she still takes the reflection for her head.Understood within conditions.Revealed too. Just now you remembered again. And what sort of appearance is it. If thus,
then elsewhere no form is displayed.Understood right now. This very one is it. Lost. And if so, there has not yet been a true time.
A little short of it.It is not the original source. Also, do not take the shadow as it. Also, in the end it is not remembered. And if so, then
it cannot be changed.

Coming from within the Correct: within nothing there is a road out of the dust. If one can only avoid violating the present taboo, it surpasses the talent of those in former times who had their tongues cut out.Gone past.A phrase within no phrase, it comes along together. Also, as for the matter from the beginning,
how is it. If so, then they do not borrow from each other.Beside this one. Already it is beside. It is naturally the same kind of person. If so, then
throughout the whole earth there is no second person.Not silence. Still more urgent than this, yet in the end not gnashing teeth. If so, then repeated instruction is not
possible.

Arrival within the Integrated: when two blades cross, there is no need to avoid it. A skilled hand is like a lotus in fire, plainly possessing a spirit that rushes to the heavens.Coming within a phrase.Host and guest do not touch each other. Each does not injure the other. Arrow after arrow
crosses in strain, the pulse continues unbroken. As for not opposing each other, if so, then they do not manage each other.It cannot be spoiled. Who is the one who gains the advantage
and who is weaker than whom. If it is thus, in the end one does not become a second person.It is not obtained from another. If it is thus, then
it is not borrowed, nor originally possessed. If it is thus, the self too does not remain, and it is not owned by the self.

Attainment within the Integrated: not falling into being or nonbeing, who dares harmonize with it? Everyone wants to get out of the current of the age, but in the end they return and sit in the ashes.subtle carrying alongnot head-on. He is a master, just fit for discussion. What is called
discussion. Bring it forth and speak, saying, asked:All wish to go beyond categories. What place is there to stand out? Yet if one moves, one dies. And if thus,
then one is at ease everywhere.it is one. Then it can be known. When one knows how to act, one cannot fool him. And again,
if it is thus, it is thanks that it is this very person. In this rank, all matters take the correct rank as primary. If it is in the correct rank, there is also no speech, and no principle of facing the guest. If it is facing the guest, that is where the biased rank reaches its limit.
This is called facing the guest. As for the mixed and inclusive mode and the like, all are different summons according to the moment. Sometimes it responds, sometimes it does not. This is also called no speech within speech, and speech within no speech. It is explained broadly in the biased and correct ranks
as there clarified. There are further words that do not enter the biased and correct ranks. Only then is it hard to speak for others. It requires a clear-eyed person to manage it, one who is not taken in by pointing east and slashing west.

cb:div.head: The Three Kinds of Falling
cb:div.p:
When a śramaṇa takes food, there are three kinds of falling. Becoming a water buffalo is the śramaṇa's falling.
Not receiving food is the noble falling. Not cutting off sounds and forms is the falling along with kinds. Simply to fall
like this belongs to what sort of person?If you want to know, this is entering among different kinds. It does not acknowledge matters on the side of the śramaṇa. Therefore the ancients provisionally borrowed the water buffalo as
a different kind. It is only a different kind in terms of affairs, not a different kind in language.

If it is the different kind within speech, then all coming and going words are kinds. Therefore
Nanquan said, Where wisdom does not reach, avoid speaking of it. If you speak of it, horns and head
arise. To call it thusness is already a transformation. One must directly move among the different kinds. Now
one must, within difference, speak and take up the matter within difference. Only when there is no speech in speech is it attained.
When Nanquan was ill, someone asked, Reverend, after a hundred years where
will you go? Nanquan said, I will go to the Yue family's place below the mountain and become a water buffalo.
The monk said, I intend to follow you, Master. May I? Nanquan said, If you follow
me, bring a stalk of grass in your mouth.This is the śramaṇa turning-body phrase. Therefore it is said: If you would approach, hold a single blade of grass in your mouth and come near him. This is called without
leakage; only then are you fit to make offerings to him.

He also said, As for falling along with kinds, right now amid all sounds, forms, and things, one turns
the body and does not follow ranks or stages. This is called falling along with kinds. He also said, As for the noble falling:
dharma-body and dharma-nature are matters on the noble side, and one must also turn away from them. This is the noble falling.
Even the white ox in the open ground is the ultimate rule of the dharma-body, yet one must still turn away from it to avoid
being stuck in a place of one color with no distinctions. All this is called matters on the side of cut-off offerings. If one wishes to make offering,
one must obtain this food. Therefore the flavor of no flavor is also called undefiled and fit
for offering. All other foods tainted by contact are not the undefiled food of liberation. Someone
asked Baizhang, What is taken as food? He said, The undefiled is food. Yunyan said, Do not
take flavor as offering. Daowu said, Where one knows and preserves this, all is offering.
Offering.

One who takes the food of right livelihood must possess all three kinds of falling. At that time a monk asked, Wearing fur
and bearing horns, what falling is that? Not cutting off sounds and forms, what falling is that? Not receiving food,
what falling is that? I said, Wearing fur and bearing horns is the śramaṇa falling. Not cutting off sounds
and forms is falling along with kinds. Not receiving food is the noble falling. The noble falling of not receiving food
is one's fundamental matter. One knows it yet does not take it, therefore it is called the noble falling. Wearing fur
and bearing horns, the śramaṇa falling, means not clinging to matters on the śramaṇa side nor to the reward-positions of the sages.
As for not cutting off sounds and forms, the falling along with kinds, when a beginner first knows his own fundamental
matter, in turning the light around he casts out forms, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and dharmas, and attains stillness.
But after the work is accomplished, he does not cling to the six dusts, falls yet is not darkened, and lets them be without obstruction. Therefore
it is said, The six teachers of the outsiders are your teachers; where those teachers fall, you also fall along. This can
be eaten. Such food is the food of right livelihood. It too is the fundamental matter. It is simply at the gates of the six faculties that seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing
are not stained; this is called falling. It is not like
the earlier fearfulness. One does not even take the fundamental matter, much less the rest.In a śramaṇa taking food there are three kinds of falls. To become
a water buffalo, what fall is that? In place of an answer it is said: Not abiding in the correct rank, not selecting his body, this is first called the śramaṇa fall. Not cutting off sounds and forms, what fall is that? In place of an answer it is said: Ordinary feelings are exhausted, and saintly measure too is forgotten.
Amid the dust of sounds and forms, one should not sever further; only then may one take food. This is called the fall of following the kind. It is also said: Where that teacher falls, you too fall along with him; only then may you take food. As for the śramaṇa fall, it is not that there is no conduct, nor is it that there is not
no interval. Though there is interval, there is always no interval. Though there is conduct, there is always no conduct. In this matter, one must certainly know the proper time and not go east or west.

Asked, What is the falling of those teachers? I said, A country old man enters the marketplace; eyes and ears,
nose, tongue, body, and mind are all lost. He asked, What is falling along with kinds? I said, Not cutting off
sounds and forms. I also said, Not losing smells and tastes. He asked, Who are those teachers? I said, The six
sense-fields. He asked, What is you also falling along? I said, Remaining. He asked, Remaining in what? I said,
Do not move or touch it, and yet do not leave sounds and forms.

Asked, Not receiving food is what falling? I said, Thoroughly understanding the right cause, not retaining a superior interpretation; therefore
it is called the noble falling.

Also Guishan said, After a hundred years, I will become a water buffalo, and on the left side of its body
there will be written one line of words: Guishan monk so-and-so. Tell me, when you see it then, what will you call it?
There was no answer. Later the master answered on his behalf, Call it a water buffalo.

Asked, I wonder, does this water buffalo know how to plow and farm? He said, Certainly. He asked, It is
of what kind? He said, One wearing fur and bearing horns. He asked, Through the four seasons, what water and grass does it eat? He said,
What does not enter the mouth. He asked, What is the water buffalo? He said, It does not realize sagehood. He asked,
What is bringing a stalk of grass in the mouth? He said, Fur and feathers are alike.
Asked, Is it beyond sages, or beyond kinds? He said, It is beyond sages.

Asked, What is the water buffalo? He said, Dark and dim, obscure and hazy. He asked, What is bringing
a stalk of grass? He said, The ancients already said it: fur and feathers are alike. He also said:
The one stalk of grass simply means clearly understanding non-change.

I said, Patriarchs and Buddhas do not know it, but the cat and the white ox do know it. Asked, Why
do the cat and the white ox know it? I said, Simply because they understand nothing at all. Asked, Then
as for patriarchs and Buddhas, why do they not know it? I said, Patriarch means holding the seal; Buddha means resemblance.
Asked, Then as for the cat and the white ox, what do they know? I said, They only know
the cat and the white ox. Asked, What is the matter the cat and white ox know? I said,
It does not come from east or west. It does not come from the thirty-two marks.
Asked, What is a patriarch? He said, There is something above. Asked, What is a Buddha? He said, A likeness.

cb:div.head: Four Kinds of Different Kinds
