cb:div.p:
First, the different kinds of coming and going: all sounds, forms, words, ranks, and stages now
abandon the father and flee away, yet all turn back upward to the ancestor and thereby become different
kinds. Also heaven, hell, hungry ghosts, animals, asuras, and the like are all different kinds.
Second, the bodhisattva the same yet different kind: first clarify oneself, then enter among the different kinds of birth-and-death
to gather and guide others. Though having realized the fruit of nirvana, one does not abandon the kinds of birth-and-death.
Benefiting oneself and benefiting others, one vows that all beings become Buddhas, and oneself becomes Buddha at the very end.
Therefore, if a great-functioning bodhisattva does not first transform sentient beings, his own matter has no way to be
accomplished. Thus Nanquan said, First pass to that side and know there is something, then come back to this side and carry on
the work. A bodhisattva possesses the six perfections and ten thousand practices. The teaching says, If even one being is not yet ferried across,
I will never accomplish perfect awakening. The vows are boundless, beings are boundless. Practicing in this way,
therefore it is called the bodhisattva same-yet-different kind. Third, the śramaṇa different kind:
first know the fundamental matter, then utterly lose all present worldly and holy causes and effects,
merits and practices. Only then does one become one with the substance itself. This is called a person who stands alone, also called a śramaṇa
of cut-off concerns. Only then are inner and outer feelings forgotten, the affairs of the three times exhausted, and one attains no remainder,
no leakage. This is called a matter on the Buddha side. It is also called pointing to heaven and earth with one hand, and also called possessing the great
śramaṇa. Turning away even from the śramaṇa's side of cut-off matters, not entering the reward-positions of the sages, only then
is it called śramaṇa practice. It is also called the śramaṇa turning the body, also called wearing fur and bearing
horns, and also called a water buffalo. Only at such a time does one enter the different kinds. It is also
called a matter on the side of form-kinds. Therefore the ancients said, The head is three feet long and the neck is two
inches long. It is only this principle and must not be understood otherwise. Fourth, the different kind within the school gate:
as Nanquan said, Where wisdom does not reach, avoid speaking of it. If you speak of it, then
horns and head arise. To call it thusness is already change. One must directly move among the different kinds
and speak the matter within the different kinds. Dongshan said, This matter must be subtly understood. The matter
lies in subtlety; the substance lies in the subtle place. I myself say, In this matter one must empty out a position
so that there is nothing fixed at all. Only when face to face there is integrated carrying can it be attained. If it is the speech of a genuine adept, it is neither
biased nor correct, neither existent nor nonexistent. This is called emptying this matter within the different kinds. One must directly
as a genuine adept set the body crosswise; on meeting wood, attach to wood; on meeting bamboo, attach to bamboo. One must guard against violation and offense. I entrust,
entrust, entrust.Someone asked me, What is different kind? I said, if I tell you, will you attain it in a donkey year? Thus someone asked Nanquan, After a hundred years, where will you go? Quan
said, To the patron family at the foot of the mountain, to become a water buffalo. He said, Can I follow the master? Quan said, If you would follow me, hold a single blade of grass in your mouth and come. I say: this water buffalo is not the same as the śramaṇa
water buffalo. You must be very careful to get it, and not lose the time.

Asked, What is the different kind that goes and comes? I said, One does not yet know one has oneself. Then I also said:
All speech, sounds, forms, and rights and wrongs are altogether the different kind that goes and comes. He asked:
What is the different kind within sameness? I said, It does not choose its body. He asked, What is furred and
horned? I said, It does not set up contact and purity. Also, when asked about contact, I did not answer in the usual way: contact is just meeting purity,
and purity is just purity. He asked, What is the different kind within the school? I said, If you want a head, then chop it
off and take it away.The former master Dongshan, because a monk asked about the śramaṇa practice, answered: The head is three chi long, the neck two cun long. He further asked me, what is the meaning of this? I said, a supreme phrase, a wondrous phrase. The monk said, called
what is a supreme phrase and wondrous phrase? I said: There are three kinds of supreme and wondrous phrases. First, when the buddhas appear in the world, for forty-nine years they establish expedients, the twelvefold teachings, hundreds of thousands of samadhis, wondrous gates each and every one penetrating
through to the end. All of these are supreme and wondrous phrases. This is speech from the side of appearing in the world. Second, from ordinary to sage, thoroughly understanding that oneself is not different from Buddha, attaining no leakage and only then attaining whole-body penetration, this is first called in a single dust mote, in a single thought, the ten
directions Bhagavans, one road to the nirvana gate. When it comes to such a time, one does not abide in the correct rank, does not choose one's body, but instead enters among different kinds, wearing fur and horns, with no different thought. Therefore it is said that all species and things cannot be compared or analogized.
They cannot be matched; all buddhas and patriarchs cannot measure it out. Therefore the ancients said: In speaking of the śramaṇa, one must not use talk of measurements with people. Therefore it is called a supreme and wondrous phrase. This is language on the side of forms and kinds. Third, all that
exists as a thing cannot be compared to it; only then is it called a supreme and wondrous phrase. Therefore the ancients said: A thousand comparisons do not match, ten thousand things cannot serve as analogy. The wise cannot know it, and those of highest capacity also do not recognize it. It is also said, originally
there is no likeness. Therefore it is a supreme and wondrous phrase. A supreme and wondrous phrase is a matter that heaven and the human world cannot measure. Therefore the ancients said it is called the phrase beyond beginning and end. Borrowing this, one speaks in terms of the side of language and kinds.

Chou the patched-robed monk asked, What is the class of form? I said, Furred and horned. He asked,
What is the class of spoken words? I said, Caoshan has only one pair of eyebrows. He asked again,
What is a water buffalo? I said, Dim and muddled. He asked, What does that mean? I
said, It does not know there is heaven and earth. Chou then brought up another case: A senior monk asked Yunju, The former master
once said, Since youth I have raised a son, his head three feet long and his neck two inches long.
What is this son raised since youth? Ju said, Daily provision is hard to forget. He asked,
What is the head three feet long? Ju said, Nothing can be done about it. He asked, What is the neck
two inches long? Ju said, Even now, can anything be done about it? He asked, What is daily
provision hard to forget? Ju said, It is just always there. He asked, What is always there? Ju
said, It is just not turning away from it. He asked, What is nothing can be done about it? Ju said, When it comes to this point,
who could do anything about it? He asked, Even now, can anything be done about it? What does this mean?
Ju said, The Buddhas of the three times can do nothing about it. He then asked me, What is the head
three feet long and the neck two inches long? I said, It is not the matter from the beginning. He asked,
What is the matter from the beginning? I said, What do you call it? He asked, What practice does a śramaṇa practice?
I said, The practice of beasts. He asked, What is the practice of beasts? I
said, Furred and horned. He asked, What is a śramaṇa? I said, Not cut off from thing after thing.
He asked, What about that which is unbroken? I said, Only then does one attain practice. He asked, What is
the person who is furred and horned? I said, One who does not fear karma. He asked, Why does one reach
such a place? I said, If one does not fear karma, where could one not arrive? He asked, I will not ask about going from ordinary
to sage. But when going from sage to ordinary, how is it? I said, A water buffalo.
He asked, What is a water buffalo? I said, Dim and muddled. He asked, What does this mean?
I said, It thinks only of water and grass; of the rest it knows nothing. He asked, What sort of thing is accomplished thereby?
I said, It is simply this: when it meets grass it eats grass, when it meets water it drinks water.It is also said, this
phrase has power. If you wish to know power, this person does not cling to matters on the śramaṇa side, and does not enter the reward-ranks of the sages; this is precisely entering different kinds. This different kind is wearing fur and horns. It is called śramaṇa practice, and also called śra-
maṇa baggage-place. It is also called the head three chi long and neck two cun long. If you wish to know this meaning, when arriving at śramaṇa practice, one does not wish to use measurements to divide near and far. One must not speak of Zhang San or Li Si. Further, head three
chi means only that from small to great, today the work is accomplished. When one reaches such a time, it is called a supreme and wondrous phrase. Neck two cun means one does not sit in the śramaṇa rank, nor abide in the reward-ranks of the sages. Therefore it is
called neck two cun long. At such a time one must not speak of fitting or not fitting. Therefore it is said: Do not bring measurements here to think about it. Even so, this is still a matter on the side of kinds. You must know there is within different kinds
a matter. Have you not seen it said: Where wisdom does not reach, one must not speak of it. Speak of it and horns immediately grow on the head. To call it thusness is already a change. One must proceed within different kinds. This is called the empty single rank, called face-to-face inclusive
carrying. It is entirely without anything fixed.

He asked, What is the kind? I said, Furred and horned. He asked, What is the difference? I
said, Doing this, doing that. He asked, What is practice? I said, If you want a head, then chop it
off and take it away.He said, As for different kinds, what sort of matter does it accomplish? I said: In this matter there are two kinds of different kinds. First, śramaṇa different kind. Second, affair-level different kind. Affair-level different kind is the civet
cat and white ox. Śramaṇa different kind means gaining freedom at every contact, and only then attaining non-change. It is not the same as that. The former master asked me, Where are you going? I said, To the place of non-change. This non-change
matter has two kinds. First, everyone fully has the matter of their own share. Second, for a person who knows there is, he does not abandon any sounds, forms, rights, or wrongs, and is not obstructed upon any and every thing. This is called unchanging in every place. It is also
called wearing fur and horns, also called entering mud and entering water, also called a fellow of baggage-carrying.

He asked, What is entering mud and entering water? The master said, No change. He asked, Is it turning the body or not?
I said, It does not turn the body. He asked, What is this person's house-affair? I said,
The sages cannot fathom it. He asked, Why can they not fathom it? I said, Because he is not
the same as the sages. He asked, This is still a matter on the side of kinds. Is there also an upward matter? I
said, There is. He asked, What is the upward matter? I said, If I tell you,
I fear it will fall to the side of kinds.

cb:div.cb:mulu: The Mysterious Pivot of the Eight Essentials
cb:div.head: The Mysterious Pivot of the Eight Essentials
cb:div.p:
Mutual interchange. No mutual interchange. Circuitous turning. Lateral participation. The pivot mechanism. Hidden functioning. Direct pressing. Lateral
raising.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Essential Instructions on the Five Positions
cb:div.head: Essential Instructions on the Five Positions
cb:div.p:
Coming within the Real is excess. The whole body alone stands revealed, the root-source of the ten thousand dharmas, without fault
and without praise. Arrival within the Partial is inner trust. Following things, it is unobstructed; like a wooden boat, inwardly empty,
empty and open, free and at ease. The Partial within the Real is compliance. Space is shattered into pieces, yet everywhere perfectly
pervading; faculties and dusts are still. The Real within the Partial is delight. The moon in water and the image in a mirror originally have no
arising or ceasing; how could there be tracks? Reaching within Integration is double clarity. The Real need not be empty;
the Partial need not be solid. No turning away, no turning toward. It is also said: the workings of mind are extinguished, form and emptiness forgotten.
There is no further concealment; the whole substance is manifest. This is called the Partial within the Real. Mountain is mountain, water
is water. No one assigns names, no thing can be compared. This is called the Real within the Partial. Bare and
naked, stark and clean, the face and features stand forth openly; filling heaven and filling earth, solely honored, without
a second. This is called Coming within the Real. It is like the Son of Heaven within the realm, not borrowing the commands of Yu, Tang, Yao,
or Shun. The eyes see and the ears hear, yet in the end borrow no other power. The ear does not enter into sound;
sound does not block the ear-root. Inside, it has just turned the body, yet amid the dust it still bears no name. This
is called Arrival within Integration. It is not mind, not object; not phenomena, not principle. From the beginning
it is apart from names and appearances; innate suchness forgets nature and characteristics. This is called Reaching within Integration.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Caoshan BenjiEnd

cb:div.p:
The layman Cizhan, styled Chanhai, was a man of Yeya Village in Tanzhou. His surname was Sugawara
clan. His secular name was Seizaemon, and his original given name was Hirotsugu. In youth he made his home in the capital
and admired Master Juecheng of Yingfeng, serving him with the rites of a disciple. Formerly he requested
me to publish the Recorded Sayings of Dongshan. That work being completed, he now publishes the Recorded Sayings of Caoshan
and appends it to what was formerly published, making a single volume, to spread the Dharma treasure widely. The merit and benefit thus
gathered he dedicates to his wife and daughter, and also uses in advance to cultivate his own blessings in the unseen.
The wife is the Chan nun Cizhao. The daughter is the virtuous woman Zhizhen.
I set forth their honored names at the right, and also record this matter, thereby transmitting the layman's
courageous deed into the undying.
Spring, first year of Kanpo, xin-you cyclical year
Respectfully recorded by the śramaṇa Xuanqi, Xuānmo of Yunzhou

app.lem: Compiled by Guo Ningzhi, the host of Wudidi

Upward
He further said. The master said, I do not say. Shan said, Why not speak

app.rdg: Fall, and even taking food, is precisely the matter of one's own share. Knowing there is, yet not taking it, therefore it is called the noble fall. If one clings to the beginner's knowing of self and to the saintly rank, therefore it is called the fall of kind. If at the beginner's first knowing of one's own matter, when turning the light around, one rejects forms, sounds, smells, tastes, touch, and dharmas and attains stillness, that is achieving merit; but afterward if one does not cling to the six dusts and such matters, and just follows one's part in dimness and lets them be, then there is obstruction. Therefore the six teachers of the outsiders are your teachers; where those teachers fall, you too fall along with them, and only then may you take food. Taking food is precisely right livelihood food. It is also right at the gates of the six faculties, in seeing, hearing, awareness, and knowing, simply not being stained by them and taking that as fall. And this is not the same as previously equalizing it with the matter of one's own share. Even that one does not take, much less the other matters. Whenever the master speaks of fall, he means they cannot be mixed and their kinds are not equal. Whenever he speaks of beginner's mind, he means that awakening and not yet awakening are the same.

app.lem:
Meng, is there still
or not
app.rdg: does the middle master still have it or not
as the monk passed his hand, the master pointed

app.lem: Asked: In receiving the teaching there is a saying
app.rdg: A monk asked about what is said in the teachings
He said, since it contains the ten thousand things
attach; the monk said, since it contains the ten thousand things, why does one whose breath is cut off not attach
one whose breath is cut off

app.lem:
He reported it to the master. The master said, Old Shishuang's voice
on hearing it made this interpretation. Therefore he showed a verse, saying: A dead tree's dragon-chant is truly seeing the Way.
A skull without consciousness, the eye first brightens. When delighting consciousness is exhausted, all traces are exhausted. How can the person
discern the clear within the turbid? The monk then again

app.rdg: lost; then he gave a verse, saying: A dead tree's dragon-chant is truly seeing the Way, a skull without consciousness, the eye first brightens; when delighting consciousness is exhausted, all traces are exhausted, how can the person discern the clear within the turbid

lem.note: The Zhong family invited him but he did not rise to go. He only wrote the Great Plum Mountain Dwelling Ode in reply.

Here the Jia edition additionally has a sixty four character exchange beginning, The master asked Elder Qiang, The Buddha true Dharma body is like empty space, responding to things and appearing in form like the moon in water, how do you speak of the principle of response, and ending with, like a well looking at a donkey.
